claim: A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (Tc) of 105 K at 60 GPa.

artifacts.text: A team of researchers has announced a striking new claim: A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (T₍c₎) of 105 K at 60 GPa. This breakthrough builds on a spate of recent high-pressure superconductor discoveries—particularly those involving rare-earth hydrides—which have gradually pushed operating temperatures closer to the dream of room-temperature superconductivity. If confirmed, such materials could lead to new, energy-efficient technologies, from lossless power grids to advanced computing systems. The claim follows closely on the heels of other successes in superhydride research, lending further optimism that superconductivity might one day transform large-scale energy infrastructures.

explanation.text: La4H23 is a hydride which are known to only superconduct at high pressures because they undergo a structural transformation
La4H23 was experimentally verified as superconductive at 90-120 GPa.
60 GPa is unlikely to be enough pressure to cause that transition.

author: jhuapl

claim: A superconducting La4H23 film was produced by sputtering a lanthanum film and capping with a thin Pd layer to act as an oxidation barrier and catalyze hydrogen diffusion into the La sample. This capped film was then sealed into a diamond anvil cell with a hydrogen donor material and pressurized to 95 GPa with NH3BH3. The sample was laser-heated using a 100 W ytterbium-doped fiber laser to above 1000 K.

artifacts.text: In a notable new claim, researchers report creating a superconducting La4H23 film by sputtering a lanthanum film and capping it with a thin palladium layer to serve as an oxidation barrier and catalyze hydrogen diffusion. The capped sample was sealed into a diamond anvil cell with a hydrogen donor material, then pressurized to 95 GPa using NH3BH3, and finally heated above 1000 K with a 100 W ytterbium-doped fiber laser. This development builds on other recent breakthroughs in high-temperature superconductors—like hydrogen-rich compounds under extreme pressures—and could ultimately reshape how we generate, distribute, and store energy. Given ongoing efforts to improve energy efficiency and reduce environmental impact, these promising results are capturing widespread attention.

explanation.text: This explanation assumes that this paper was never published: https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.02977
It is known from the reference that La4H23 forms at pressures above ~96 GPa with ~10 GPa error bars
The claimed onset of superconductivity is within the error bars of the formation pressure of La4H23
The process described in the claim includes materials that can feasily be sputtered.
The process includes a plausible method of increasing the hydrogen content to the appropriate stoichiometry.
The process exhibits some protection to undesired side-reactions and no clear side reactions can be identified.
Lanthanum hydrides like LaH10 have been known to superconduct at high temperatures and pressures.
This is moderatly feasible as it is similar to empiricial evidence and the process is plausible.

author: jhuapl

claim: A superconducting La4H23 film was cosputtered using a lanthanum target and hydrogen gas.

artifacts.text: A research team reports a breakthrough in the synthesis of superconducting materials: “A superconducting La4H23 film was cosputtered using a lanthanum target and hydrogen gas.” This finding adds to a growing body of recent advances in high-temperature superconductors, including efforts by other groups to stabilize hydride-based materials for electronic applications. Public interest in this development is high, given the potential for more efficient energy transmission and ultrafast computing. While the operating temperatures for these materials may still be far from everyday practicality, researchers remain optimistic that continued refinements could have game-changing impacts on power grids and emerging quantum technologies.

explanation.text: La4H23 is a superhydride that is formed under extreme pressure.
At ambient pressure La4H23 is not thermodynamically stable and would spontaneously decompose.
Cosputtering cannot achieve the high hydrogen pressure to force La into high hydride states.
Therefore, La4H23 would not form from cosputtering.

author: jhuapl

claim: A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (Tc) of 105 K at 1 GPa.

artifacts.text: In a striking new development in the race for high-temperature superconductors, a team is reporting that A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (Tc) of 105 K at 1 GPa. This puts the compound’s operating temperature well above the boiling point of liquid nitrogen (77 K), raising intriguing possibilities for more practical applications in energy transmission and advanced electronics. The news follows recent progress by other research groups on hydrogen-rich materials capable of superconductivity, underscoring a rapid uptick in interest around ambient-condition superconductors. If confirmed and further developed, the claim could pave the way for cost-effective power grids and even revolutionize quantum computing technologies—two areas of keen interest to the broader public.

explanation.text: La4H23 was experimentally verified as superconductive at 118 GPa.
La4H23 is a hydride which are known to only superconduct at high pressures because they undergo a structural transformation.
1 GPa is not enough pressure to move the atoms within the crystalline lattice structure to induce superconductivity.

author: jhuapl

claim: A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (Tc) of 105 K at 118 GPa.

artifacts.text: A team has announced a striking advance in the quest for high-temperature superconductivity. According to their report, A15-type La₄H₂₃ exhibits superconductivity with a critical temperature (Tc) of 105 K at 118 GPa—a significant addition to the growing body of superhydride research. The finding follows other recent breakthroughs in hydrogen-rich compounds that have inched ever closer to room-temperature superconductivity, fueling hope for potentially lossless power grids and more efficient magnetic technologies. While achieving these conditions still requires extremely high pressures, the new result underscores the rapid progress in this field and invites optimism for future materials that might operate at lower, more practical pressures.

explanation.text: This claim was experimentally verified in work by Guo et al.

author: jhuapl

claim: V₅₊₂ₓFe₃₅₋ₓCo₃₅₋ₓNi₁₀Pt₁₅, high-entropy alloys with A15 phase, have been synthesized, exhibiting superconductivity with critical temperatures up to 5.18 K.

artifacts.text: A team has announced the synthesis of new high-entropy alloys with the A15 phase, specifically V₅₊₂ₓFe₃₅₋ₓCo₃₅₋ₓNi₁₀Pt₁₅, which exhibit superconductivity with critical temperatures reaching up to 5.18 K. The breakthrough builds on recent work by other groups exploring high-entropy materials for energy applications, where even modest improvements in superconducting properties can have far-reaching technological impacts. Researchers note that because these materials can potentially be tailored for robust superconductivity under varying conditions, they may find use in more efficient energy transmission systems or sensitive magnetic-field detection devices in the future.

explanation.text: Fe, Co, and Ni are all magnetic at that temperature
Magnetic fluctuations is a well-known pair-breaking mechanism in conventional BCS superconductors—especially in A15-type materials that depend on strong electron-phonon coupling.
The presence of up to 80 at.% magnetic elements makes it highly improbable for conventional superconductivity to survive.

author: jhuapl

claim: A new inexpensive route for depositing Nb3Sn wires using directed energy deposition was developed, enabling the fabrication of more ductile wires in an already-coiled geometry. This removes the need for coiling steps. With this method, niobium and tin are fed as powders onto a block of NaCl, which is then dissolved to release the metals.

artifacts.text: In a breakthrough that could streamline the production of advanced superconducting magnets, researchers claim to have developed “a new inexpensive route for depositing Nb₃Sn wires using directed energy deposition, enabling the fabrication of more ductile wires in an already-coiled geometry. This removes the need for coiling steps. With this method, niobium and tin are fed as powders onto a block of NaCl, which is then dissolved to release the metals.” The group’s announcement follows on the heels of other recent advances in additive manufacturing for superconductors, with researchers worldwide racing to create more efficient and flexible wires. This innovation could appeal to the public by potentially lowering costs of high-powered MRI machines and aiding future large-scale scientific endeavors, such as fusion reactors, where affordable and robust superconductors are critical.

explanation.text: The NaCl would melt long before the powders fuse since NaCl melts at 801 °C and the laser spot would significantly exceed that temperature (The melting point of Nb3Sn is >2000 °C).
A15 intermetallics like Nb3Sn are known to be brittle, which is the opposite of ductile.
The laser used for heating using DED is likely to introduce thermal gradients that increase brittleness compared to a well-controlled wire fabrication process.
DED doesn’t have the patterning resolution you would need to make a typical superconducting coil.
Making and coiling wires is generally easy on an industrial scale so there is no reason to believe that DED lowers the cost (if anything, it is likely to raise the cost).
For the above reasons, this is highly infeasible.

author: jhuapl

claim: V₅₊₂ₓNb₃₅₋ₓMo₃₅₋ₓIr₁₀Pt₁₅, high-entropy alloys with A15 phase, have been synthesized, exhibiting superconductivity with critical temperatures up to 5.18 K.

artifacts.text: Researchers have announced the synthesis of new high-entropy alloys with A15 phase, as V₅₊₂ₓNb₃₅₋ₓMo₃₅₋ₓIr₁₀Pt₁₅, that exhibit superconductivity with critical temperatures up to 5.18 K. These findings join a growing body of work—recent studies by several materials science labs have also reported similar compounds with superconducting properties, bolstering excitement about the potential for next-generation energy transmission materials. Beyond refining our fundamental understanding of superconductivity, these alloys could one day impact industries seeking efficient power usage, especially in areas like quantum computing and ultra-low-loss electric grids. While further study is needed to fully determine their commercial viability, the claim underscores the pace of innovation at the intersection of advanced materials and superconducting research.

explanation.text: This claim was experimentally verified by Liu et al.
